system_prompt:
You are extracting load-bearing passages from one chapter of a guitar method book.

The user will give you the verbatim text of one chapter. Extract every passage
that does any of the following:

- teaches a technique (how to play, fingering, hand position, picking pattern)
- defines a concept (a chord type, a scale, a position system, a terminology)
- gives a rule (when to do X, what to avoid, what to prefer)
- shows a load-bearing example (an exercise that illustrates a method's
  characteristic move)

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  { "quotes": [
      { "topic": "<short topic label>",
        "verbatim_quote": "<exact substring of the input>",
        "why_load_bearing": "<one sentence on why this passage matters>",
        "page": <int, optional>
      },
      ...
  ] }

CRITICAL RULES:
1. `verbatim_quote` MUST be an EXACT substring of the input text. Do not
   paraphrase. Do not add words. Do not fix typos. Copy character-for-character.
2. If you can't find a substantial passage in the input that fits a topic,
   skip the topic — don't fabricate.
3. Skip prose that is purely biographical, motivational, or transitional.
   Keep prose that teaches.
4. Aim for 3-15 quotes per chapter. A chapter with only one load-bearing
   passage gets one quote; a dense chapter gets many.
5. `topic` is a short label (2-6 words). Use the book's own terminology
   when possible.
6. `why_load_bearing` is one sentence in YOUR voice explaining the
   significance — not a paraphrase of the quote.
user_prompt:
Chapter 10: Jazz Standard Study #9: All the Things You Are
Pages 149-167 of the book.

<<<CHAPTER TEXT

| have to admit, I’m particularly excited to explore this study with you
because All the Things You Are is one of my favorite jazz standards. |
personally enjoy improvising over it and have enjoyed playing it in many
different ways.

I've played it as a ballad, at medium tempos, and at blazing fast
speeds. I’ve played it in 4/4, 3/4, and even 7/8. I’ve played it in time
and rubato. I’ve played it solo, in duos, trios, quartets, and quintets. |
think you get my point.

However, we aren't studying this song just because | have a bias to-
wards it. | often use this song as a teaching tool because it has many
musical lessons packed into the harmony. Most notably, it’s a perfect
study of cycling 4ths, which as we know, is an important movement in

jazz repertoire.
Al the Things You Are thie

All the Things You Are was written by Jerome Kern with lyrics by Oscar Ham-
merstein Il. It was composed for the 1939 musical “Very Warm in May.” It was
later featured in the 1944 film “Broadway Rhythm.” This jazz standard has an
AABA form and cycles through five different key centers. It is most commonly
played in the key of concert Ab major. It has a slightly longer 36 bar form as
opposed to a more standard 32 bar form.

That’s right. Five different key centers. We'll surely be exploring that
in our chords analysis. But as per usual, let’s do some listening first.
There are so many different recordings of this, both old and new. I’m
going to include a few extra suggestions than | normally do, because |
want you to see some of the possibilities this song has to offer.


>> SUGGESTED LISTENING

Ella Fitzgerald from “Sings the Jerome Kern
Songbook” 1982

Charlie Parker from “On Dial: the Complete
Sessions” 1993

Clifford Brown from “The Clifford Brown Sextet in
Paris” 2014 (Release)

Brad Mehldau from “Art of the Trio 4” 1999

Keith Jarrett from “Standards Vol. 1” 1983

Django Reinhardt from “The Indispensable Django
Reinhardt” 1949-1950

Again, these recordings are just scratching the surface. | purposefully
included some more modern recordings from Brad Mehldau and Keith
Jarrett. Note that Brad Mehldau’s version is in 7/8 time as opposed
to 4/4. It’s a brilliant recording and demonstrates some fantastic pos-
sibilities over this tune.

Now let’s dive into the chord map, guide tones, and chords analysis.

You probably noticed the intro on the lead sheet. This is the classic
intro to All the Things You Are, and you will hear it played on different
recordings. However, it’s not always included, and you certainly don’t
have to. It is an interesting set of chords, but the idea is the C7(#9)
acts as the V chord into the top of the form.

Similar to our study of Have You Met Miss Jones, | have color coded
each section by key centers. As mentioned before, there are five dif-
ferent key centers at play, and so it’s important to mark them off.

Blue = Ab major (parent key)

For the sake of analyzing these chords clearly, | haven’t labeled the
key centers in the roman numerals. Rather, the colors are distinguish-
ing them, so pay attention to the colors.

There’s a lot to unpack here, so let’s start with the first 8 bars.

Fun? Bouin? ad Aas?
vi ii Vv I

Duns G’ Cua’

THE JAZZ STANDARDS PLAYBOOK 158 |



The first key center we are dealing with is the parent key, Ab major.
Notice that it starts with the vi chord. This vi chord starts a long cycle
of 4ths all the way through the Dpmaj7 in bar 5. Everything here is
diatonically pure to the key center. No minor chords being subbed for
dominant chords or any other kind of stretches.

It’s in bar 6 where we make our first transition into a new key center.
The basic chord change in bar 6 is a simple G7 which acts as a V chord
moving into Cmaj7, the new | chord. However, sometimes musicians
will add aii in front of it (Dmin7), making it a short ii-V-I. Where there is
a V chord, you can almost always add a ii in front of it.

It’s hard to say why Jerome Kern made this compositional choice oth-
er than it sounds good in context with the melody and within a V-I pro-
gression. If you do add the ii in front of the V, it’s a nice half step bass
motion from the Domaj7 to the Dmin7. For memorization purposes,
you can think of the Dpmaj7 as being a tritone interval away from the
G7, but it’s easiest just to memorize the key center being targeted.

Let’s take a look at the next section.

Cumin’ Fain’ BY” E?was”

THE JAZZ STANDARDS PLAYBOOK



The Cmaj7 in bar 8 is transformed into a Cmin7 in bar 9. Why? Be-
cause now we are moving into a new key center: Eb major.

What | want you to pay special attention to here is that bars 9-16 are
literally an exact replica of the first 8 bars of the tune. The only differ-
ence here is we are starting in a different key. But if you look at the
roman numeral analysis, everything is exactly the same. We start on
the vi chord, end on the IV chord in bar 13, and then move to the next
key center in bar 14. The next key center (G major) has the same re-
lationship to the previous key as C major had to Ab major. This time |
have explicitly added the ii chord in front of the V in bar 14.

This is all very fascinating to me. Jerome Kern brilliantly added new
melodic movement and color simply by repeating himself in a different
key. Once you understand what’s happening here, this song immedi-
ately gets less complicated to understand.

In bar 17, which officially marks what is considered the B section, the
key center remains in G major. | think this is brilliant as well. It breaks up
the continual flow of chord changes and offers something new to add
a different texture to the piece.

Let’s take a look at the next section.

Faun? B’ Ema” cu
2 ii V I V of vi

Now for a curveball. It’s a new key center, and this time it’s E major. We
have a half step bass movement from Gmaj7 in bar 20 into F#min7 in
bar 21, which functions as the ii chord in E major.

Again, why Jerome Kern chose this key center, I’m not sure. But it
sounds awesome!

The C7alt (meaning it has some kind of altered extension in it) in bar
24 is back in the key of Ab major. Why? It is the V of vi. It’s acting as
the V of the relative minor, but the Fmin7 in bar 25 is clearly not func-
tioning as ai chord. It’s a vi chord. Hence why | have colored it blue.

THE JAZZ STANDARDS PLAYBOOK 160 |



A way to help you remember this chord is to think, “the bridge is end-
ing and there is one more A section to go. How do | get back home?”
Easy. Insert V chord here. The V chord is always your secret weapon
for resolving to a new key center.

Let’s take a look at the last section of importance.

Downs” Gis Cun’ Bow?
Dire reise rs ieee ries se)
” IV IV7 of IV iii biti dim
Bryn? po Aun?
ORI TT AAT ATES AIST
* ii V ] ~~ 2-2-2022 n nner nner anne ee

Bars 25-28 are just repeats of the first 4 bars of the song. But this is
where this last A section differs from the first A.

| do think the easiest way to think about this section is to conclude it
is all in the key of Ab major. However, I’ve outlined bars 29-32 because
clearly there is some non-diatonic stuff going on here.

Dbmaj7 is the IV chord. No issues there. But the next chord is not so
clear. It’s important to note that some charts or musicians will play
a Dbmin9 here. Not a problem. We’ve seen making the IV into a mi-
nor iv before in our All of Me study. Or other times it will be treated as
a ii-V (Dbmin9-Gb73). | have this chord labeled as a Gb13 (the 13" is
the melody note), and the best way to describe it in roman numerals is
a IV7 of IV. Whether you choose to play a minor iv or the Gb13, don’t
get too hung up on it. It’s a movement of a 4%, which as we know plays
quite nice in jazz harmony.

In bar 31 we have the iii chord. Again, nothing odd there. But then we
move to the biii diminished. What’s going on here?

THE JAZZ STANDARDS PLAYBOOK 161 |



In our Have You Met Miss Jones study we identified the term passing
diminished chord. In Miss Jones, the passing diminished chord was a
substitute for the VI7 chord. In this case, the biii diminished isn’t nec-
essarily replacing anything. Its primary job is to connect the ili chord to
the ii chord chromatically.

Think of bars 29-32 this way: The IV chord needs to connect to the
ii chord. There are many different ways to do that, but Jerome Kern
chose to do it this way. The result is some beautiful harmonic move-
ment that departs from the cycling 4ths solution of connecting them
(ii-V’s). The song ends with a simple ii-V-I in the parent key.

| know that was a lot to digest. Feel free to take a deep breath and
read through some of this again while relating everything to the chords
analysis chart.


IMPROV LESSON

How do we connect these chords together in our improvisation? That’s
the next natural question, whether you were previously familiar with
this song or not.

Throughout this book, | have been providing you with lots of tools. Let’s
go through some of them to serve as a fresh reminder:

1. Guide tones. | believe guide tones are helpful to connect chord
changes together. That’s why I’ve mapped them out for you in
every lesson.

2. Use the melody. Not sure which notes to choose? Start with the
melody, and incorporate it into what you're playing.

3. Use the chord tones. The root and 5" are up for grabs too. Map
out all of the chord tones. It can help you identify more note
choices to work with.

4. Write a contrafact. Take a step back and slow the improvisa-
tion process down. Compose your own simple melody so you can
begin to discover how to turn note choices into musical phrases.

5. Use motifs. Sometimes creating rhythmic and melodic patterns
over tricky passages can help you simplify things and develop
themes.

6. Write your own licks, or transcribe them. Break the song down
into smaller parts and compose licks over different chord pro-
gressions. Even better: learn from the best and grab some licks
from your favorite jazz musicians. Go straight to the records.


Combine these with having a solid knowledge of navigating ii-V-l’s,
and you’re on the right track.

For me, one of the most powerful practices is composing my own
solos. This is similar in concept to writing a contrafact, except this
time | am expanding from a simple melody.

My goal when composing solos is to slow the improvisation process
down and give myself the space to dream up precisely what | would
like to improvise. It’s incredible to me that when | have done this, it has
helped me solidify the jazz language | know.

I’ve given this assignment to private students and every single time |
get the same response: Wow. That really helped.

Now, if you are a beginner and new to jazz music, you may be getting
intimidated. Don’t be! Recently | stumbled across a solo | had com-
posed my freshman year of college, and | played through it. It was
rough. But the funny thing is | remember at the time feeling like | had
written a solo | wanted to take. This helped me realize how much I’ve
improved over the years.

No matter what level you are at, you can do this. It doesn’t have to
be phenomenal, and it doesn’t have to sound perfect. It just has to be
what you know right now. It will help you solidify that. You don’t have
to write it down. Just compose it on your instrument and memorize it.

Here’s a simple solo | composed over All the Things You Are with the
intention of spelling out the chord changes very clearly. Play through it
for yourself.

This solo should help you get some ideas for improvising over All the
Things You Are. Feel free to take anything you like from there. There
is no such thing as stealing when it comes to jazz (at least not in the
negative sense)!

If you’re feeling up to the challenge, join me and compose your own
solo. Even just the effort of doing so will help you. As I’ve said before,
the best way to become a better jazz improviser is just to do it. Com-
posing takes some of the pressure off, and will, in turn, help you when
it comes to actually creating in the moment.

Now on to our last jazz standard study of this book: Stella by Starlight.

167


CHAPTER TEXT>>>
